summary: Joe Reis describes how AI coding assistants have revitalized his enthusiasm for programming by dramatically accelerating iteration cycles and reducing the friction of getting stuck. While embracing these tools, he cautions against mindless 'vibe coding' in production environments, warning that prototypes quickly become production code and that understanding what the AI generates remains essential.
key_points: AI assistants have transformed coding from a weeks-long slog into rapid, dopamine-fueled iteration
The key benefit is using AI as a 'rubber ducky' for design discussions and quick scaffold generation
Knowing what you're building and understanding the generated code remains crucial
Vibe coding is dangerous because 'there's usually no such thing as a prototype' - working code becomes production code
Expects a 'Great Pacific Garbage Patch of AI Slopware' as a consequence of careless AI-assisted development
money_quotes: What might have taken weeks or months to build can be done in hours or days. Got a crazy idea? Fire up AI and try to create it.
My concern with vibe coding is there's usually no such thing as a 'prototype.' The moment your prototype works in the wild, it's in production.
We will have the equivalent of the Great Pacific Garbage Patch of AI Slopware very soon.
It puzzles me that this is controversial, but given how crazy the world is, I shouldn't be shocked.
key_insight: AI coding tools have made programming genuinely enjoyable again by eliminating friction, but their power demands that developers maintain understanding of what they're building to avoid contributing to an inevitable wave of AI-generated slopware.
